title: Pyoderma Gangrenosum
oneLiner: Painful, rapidly progressive neutrophilic ulcerating skin disease – strongly associated with IBD, arthritis, haematological disorders; pathergy triggers lesions; requires prompt immunosuppression.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Septic shock from secondary infection** – PG ulcers often superinfected; if febrile, hypotensive, or spreading cellulitis/crepitus → start IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics immediately (e.g., co‑amoxiclav + metronidazole) AND cultures BEFORE escalation of immunosuppression.
- **Toxic megacolon (if underlying IBD)** – severe abdominal distension, pain, tenderness, fever, tachypnoea, hypotension; AXR shows >6 cm transverse colon; call surgical team and gastroenterology STAT – do NOT give high‑dose steroids without surgical review.
- **Rapid progression of PG to systemic neutrophilic disorder** – rare, but if fever, arthralgia, neutrophilia, and expanding ulceration occur together → suspect Sweet’s syndrome overlap or haematological crisis (e.g., myelodysplasia); urgent blood film, LDH, and haematology referral.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** severe localised pain (out of proportion to ulcer size); rapidly enlarging ulcer over days–weeks; history of minor trauma or surgery at site (pathergy); constitutional symptoms (fever, malaise) in 30–50%.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Ulcer with **violaceous, undermined, raised border** (classic “rolled” edge)
  - **Necrotic base** with purulent exudate (often sterile)
  - **Surrounding erythema** with no induration
  - **Multiple lesions** possible – often on lower legs, trunk, peristomal areas
  - **Pathergy test** – slight pressure with a sterile needle at edge produces new pustule after 24–48 hours (supportive but not diagnostic)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Infection** (acute bacterial, atypical mycobacteria, deep fungal) – most critical to exclude; histology shows organisms, neutrophilic abscesses; culture positive; no response to steroids
- **Vasculitis** (e.g., ANCA‑associated, polyarteritis nodosa) – palpable purpura, systemic features, positive ANCA/ANA; biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis
- **Malignancy** (squamous cell carcinoma, cutaneous lymphoma) – chronic ulcer with indurated rolled edge; biopsy shows malignant cells; consider in non‑healing chronic ulcers
- **Venous / arterial ulcer** – pain improves with elevation (venous) or worse with dependency (arterial); no violaceous undermined border; peripheral pulses reduced; ABPI abnormal
- **Martorell hypertensive ischaemic ulcer** – very painful, symmetrical, on lateral leg; no undermined border; associated with severe hypertension; biopsy shows arteriolosclerosis

**Splitter:** **Biopsy from the ulcer edge** – neutrophilic infiltrate without vasculitis or organisms → PG. If organisms seen → infection. If vasculitis → reject PG.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full skin inspection for other lesions/pathergy
  - Swab of ulcer base for bacterial culture & sensitivity (and fungal culture if atypical)
  - Anal inspection + PR exam (if IBD suspected)
  - Vital signs + NEWS2
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (neutrophilia common), CRP, ESR
  - Renal function, LFTs, albumin
  - Iron studies, B12/folate (look for malabsorption)
  - Autoimmune screen: ANA, ANCA, anti‑CCP (if symmetrical polyarthritis)
  - Stool cultures + C. diff toxin (if diarrhoea ± IBD)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Plain AXR if abdominal pain/distension (exclude toxic megacolon)
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis if suspected IBD not previously diagnosed (colonic wall thickening, extra‑intestinal findings)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Skin biopsy** – punch biopsy from the **edge** of the ulcer (including subcutaneous fat) sent for histology + tissue culture (bacterial, mycobacterial, fungal). Histology: dense neutrophilic dermal infiltrate, no vasculitis.
  - Colonoscopy + biopsies (if undiagnosed IBD) – but defer in acute abdomen.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Su (2018) criteria – major: rapid progression, classic morphology, histology (neutrophilic infiltrate + negative culture), response to immunosuppression; minor: pathergy, associated systemic disease. ≥2 major + ≥1 minor for definite.
- **Never-miss test:** **Punch biopsy for histology AND tissue culture** – omitting it risks treating infection as PG (with steroids → sepsis) or treating PG as infection (with antibiotics → progression). Always do both.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - Secure airway, breathing, circulation if septic
  - Blood cultures, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics if signs of infection (e.g., co‑amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS) – but avoid prolonged antibiotics without ruling out infection.
  - **Pain control** – IV morphine if severe (titrate to pain score)
  - **Wound care** – **do NOT debride** (pathergy worsens ulcer); apply non‑adherent dressing (e.g., silicone mesh) with normal saline; no wet‑to‑dry gauze.
  - **Systemic steroids** – start once infection is reasonably excluded (negative Gram stain/culture, no fever, no spreading cellulitis). Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg PO (max 60 mg/day) or IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg/day for severe cases (but call senior first).
  - Contact dermatology and/or gastroenterology if underlying IBD suspected.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Monitor ulcer size daily (measure, photograph)
  - Taper steroids after 7–10 days if improving (reduce by 5–10 mg every 1–2 weeks)
  - Consider steroid‑sparing agent early: **cyclosporine** 3–5 mg/kg/day PO in two divided doses (check BP, renal function, drug levels); or **infliximab** 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks (if severe/refractory)
  - Wound care: topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment BD over ulcer edge (not on necrotic base), or topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment BD (if small superficial)
  - Antibiotics – stop if cultures negative and no clinical infection; continue if proven infection (duration guided by cultures)
  - **Nutritional support** – high‑protein diet; consider NG feeding if IBD with weight loss
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Treat underlying disease (IBD, arthritis, haematological)
  - Maintenance immunosuppression (low‑dose prednisolone ± azathioprine/methotrexate if steroid‑sparing)
  - Wound care: keep clean, dry; avoid any trauma; use barrier creams near stoma if peristomal
  - Refer to dermatology, rheumatology, gastroenterology as needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h if nil by mouth (or 1 L over 8 h then review)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., tinzaparin 3500 units SC OD) if not high bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal oral diet if tolerated; NBM only if toxic megacolon/surgery suspected
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then 4‑hourly; if NEWS2 ≥3 → increase frequency to 1‑hourly
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line:** Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg PO daily (max 60 mg/day) – wean after 7–10 days; for IV pulse: methylprednisolone 500 mg IV daily for 3 days.
- **Alternatives:**
  - Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day PO in two divided doses – monitor trough levels (aim 100–200 ng/mL), BP, creatinine
  - Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, then 6‑weekly – for severe/refractory PG
  - Dapsone 50–100 mg/day PO (for milder cases) – check G6PD first
  - Topical: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment BD; tacrolimus 0.1% ointment BD
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Cyclosporine: avoid with grapefruit juice; interacts with azoles, macrolides, statins
  - Infliximab: contraindicated in active TB, severe infections; screen for latent TB before use
  - Prednisolone: caution in diabetes, hypertension, peptic ulcer disease; cannot stop abruptly
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cyclosporine – reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min; avoid if severe hepatic impairment
  - Prednisolone – no dose adjustment needed, but monitor glucose
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting high‑dose steroids without first ruling out infection** – can precipitate fulminant sepsis. Always send cultures + biopsy first. If in doubt, start broad‑spectrum antibiotics and delay steroids until culture results (usually 48 h).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial infection → cellulitis, abscess, sepsis
  - Pathergic extension of ulcer (debridement, surgery, trauma) → deep necrosis
  - Toxic megacolon (if active IBD)
  - Steroid side‑effects: hyperglycaemia, hypertension, psychosis, GI bleed
- **Chronic:**
  - Scarring, contractures, disfigurement
  - Recurrent disease (up to 40% relapse rate)
  - Development of underlying haematological malignancy (monitor FBC)
sections.Prognosis:
- Most cases respond to systemic immunosuppression within 1–3 weeks; ulcers heal over weeks–months.
- Mortality low (<5%) but serious complications (sepsis, perforation) increase risk. Chronic course common if underlying disease untreated.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do a punch biopsy from the ulcer edge (including undermined border) for both histology and tissue culture – this is the cornerstone of diagnosis.**
- **Always consider underlying IBD or haematological disease – take a thorough history (bowel symptoms, blood, weight loss, joint pain, fever, night sweats) and check FBC, inflammatory markers, iron studies even if asymptomatic.**
- **Avoid any debridement, surgical excision, or even aggressive dressing changes – pathergy will worsen the ulcer dramatically. Use non‑adherent dressings and minimal handling.**
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT start high‑dose steroids before excluding infection – you risk turning a localised infection into life‑threatening sepsis.**
- **Do NOT diagnose PG simply because the ulcer “looks typical” – always biopsy and culture; mimics (especially infection and malignancy) are common.**
- **Do NOT manage PG without involving dermatology and, if IBD present, gastroenterology – this is a complex disease requiring multidisciplinary care.**
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** Ulcer rapidly enlarging (measure daily), new pus/fever/chills, severe pain not controlled, inability to tolerate oral intake, any sign of abdominal distension or change in bowel habit.
- **Follow‑up:** Dermatology within 1 week; if new‑onset IBD suspected – gastroenterology within 2 weeks. Check bloods weekly while on cyclosporine/prednisolone.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, rapid ulcer expansion (>1 cm/day), fever >38.5°C without clear source, or any need for IV antibiotics.
- **Call ICU if:** Septic shock refractory to fluids/vasopressors, or toxic megacolon requiring surgical decompression.
- **Activate crash team for:** GCS <13, respiratory rate >35, systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 2 L IV fluid bolus.

rawMarkdown:
## Pyoderma Gangrenosum

**One-liner:** Painful, rapidly progressive neutrophilic ulcerating skin disease – strongly associated with IBD, arthritis, haematological disorders; pathergy triggers lesions; requires prompt immunosuppression.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Septic shock from secondary infection** – PG ulcers often superinfected; if febrile, hypotensive, or spreading cellulitis/crepitus → start IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics immediately (e.g., co‑amoxiclav + metronidazole) AND cultures BEFORE escalation of immunosuppression.
- **Toxic megacolon (if underlying IBD)** – severe abdominal distension, pain, tenderness, fever, tachypnoea, hypotension; AXR shows >6 cm transverse colon; call surgical team and gastroenterology STAT – do NOT give high‑dose steroids without surgical review.
- **Rapid progression of PG to systemic neutrophilic disorder** – rare, but if fever, arthralgia, neutrophilia, and expanding ulceration occur together → suspect Sweet’s syndrome overlap or haematological crisis (e.g., myelodysplasia); urgent blood film, LDH, and haematology referral.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** severe localised pain (out of proportion to ulcer size); rapidly enlarging ulcer over days–weeks; history of minor trauma or surgery at site (pathergy); constitutional symptoms (fever, malaise) in 30–50%.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Ulcer with **violaceous, undermined, raised border** (classic “rolled” edge)
  - **Necrotic base** with purulent exudate (often sterile)
  - **Surrounding erythema** with no induration
  - **Multiple lesions** possible – often on lower legs, trunk, peristomal areas
  - **Pathergy test** – slight pressure with a sterile needle at edge produces new pustule after 24–48 hours (supportive but not diagnostic)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Infection** (acute bacterial, atypical mycobacteria, deep fungal) – most critical to exclude; histology shows organisms, neutrophilic abscesses; culture positive; no response to steroids
- **Vasculitis** (e.g., ANCA‑associated, polyarteritis nodosa) – palpable purpura, systemic features, positive ANCA/ANA; biopsy shows leukocytoclastic vasculitis
- **Malignancy** (squamous cell carcinoma, cutaneous lymphoma) – chronic ulcer with indurated rolled edge; biopsy shows malignant cells; consider in non‑healing chronic ulcers
- **Venous / arterial ulcer** – pain improves with elevation (venous) or worse with dependency (arterial); no violaceous undermined border; peripheral pulses reduced; ABPI abnormal
- **Martorell hypertensive ischaemic ulcer** – very painful, symmetrical, on lateral leg; no undermined border; associated with severe hypertension; biopsy shows arteriolosclerosis

**Splitter:** **Biopsy from the ulcer edge** – neutrophilic infiltrate without vasculitis or organisms → PG. If organisms seen → infection. If vasculitis → reject PG.

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
  - Full skin inspection for other lesions/pathergy
  - Swab of ulcer base for bacterial culture & sensitivity (and fungal culture if atypical)
  - Anal inspection + PR exam (if IBD suspected)
  - Vital signs + NEWS2
- **Bloods:**
  - FBC (neutrophilia common), CRP, ESR
  - Renal function, LFTs, albumin
  - Iron studies, B12/folate (look for malabsorption)
  - Autoimmune screen: ANA, ANCA, anti‑CCP (if symmetrical polyarthritis)
  - Stool cultures + C. diff toxin (if diarrhoea ± IBD)
  - Blood cultures if febrile
- **Imaging:**
  - **First-line:** Plain AXR if abdominal pain/distension (exclude toxic megacolon)
  - **Second-line:** CT abdomen/pelvis if suspected IBD not previously diagnosed (colonic wall thickening, extra‑intestinal findings)
- **Special tests:**
  - **Skin biopsy** – punch biopsy from the **edge** of the ulcer (including subcutaneous fat) sent for histology + tissue culture (bacterial, mycobacterial, fungal). Histology: dense neutrophilic dermal infiltrate, no vasculitis.
  - Colonoscopy + biopsies (if undiagnosed IBD) – but defer in acute abdomen.
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Su (2018) criteria – major: rapid progression, classic morphology, histology (neutrophilic infiltrate + negative culture), response to immunosuppression; minor: pathergy, associated systemic disease. ≥2 major + ≥1 minor for definite.
- **Never-miss test:** **Punch biopsy for histology AND tissue culture** – omitting it risks treating infection as PG (with steroids → sepsis) or treating PG as infection (with antibiotics → progression). Always do both.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour/ED):**
  - Secure airway, breathing, circulation if septic
  - Blood cultures, IV broad‑spectrum antibiotics if signs of infection (e.g., co‑amoxiclav 1.2 g IV TDS + metronidazole 500 mg IV TDS) – but avoid prolonged antibiotics without ruling out infection.
  - **Pain control** – IV morphine if severe (titrate to pain score)
  - **Wound care** – **do NOT debride** (pathergy worsens ulcer); apply non‑adherent dressing (e.g., silicone mesh) with normal saline; no wet‑to‑dry gauze.
  - **Systemic steroids** – start once infection is reasonably excluded (negative Gram stain/culture, no fever, no spreading cellulitis). Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg PO (max 60 mg/day) or IV methylprednisolone 500–1000 mg/day for severe cases (but call senior first).
  - Contact dermatology and/or gastroenterology if underlying IBD suspected.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**
  - Monitor ulcer size daily (measure, photograph)
  - Taper steroids after 7–10 days if improving (reduce by 5–10 mg every 1–2 weeks)
  - Consider steroid‑sparing agent early: **cyclosporine** 3–5 mg/kg/day PO in two divided doses (check BP, renal function, drug levels); or **infliximab** 5 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 weeks (if severe/refractory)
  - Wound care: topical clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment BD over ulcer edge (not on necrotic base), or topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment BD (if small superficial)
  - Antibiotics – stop if cultures negative and no clinical infection; continue if proven infection (duration guided by cultures)
  - **Nutritional support** – high‑protein diet; consider NG feeding if IBD with weight loss
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**
  - Treat underlying disease (IBD, arthritis, haematological)
  - Maintenance immunosuppression (low‑dose prednisolone ± azathioprine/methotrexate if steroid‑sparing)
  - Wound care: keep clean, dry; avoid any trauma; use barrier creams near stoma if peristomal
  - Refer to dermatology, rheumatology, gastroenterology as needed
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h if nil by mouth (or 1 L over 8 h then review)
  - VTE prophylaxis: LMWH (e.g., tinzaparin 3500 units SC OD) if not high bleeding risk
  - Diet: normal oral diet if tolerated; NBM only if toxic megacolon/surgery suspected
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then 4‑hourly; if NEWS2 ≥3 → increase frequency to 1‑hourly

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line:** Prednisolone 0.5–1 mg/kg PO daily (max 60 mg/day) – wean after 7–10 days; for IV pulse: methylprednisolone 500 mg IV daily for 3 days.
- **Alternatives:**
  - Cyclosporine 3–5 mg/kg/day PO in two divided doses – monitor trough levels (aim 100–200 ng/mL), BP, creatinine
  - Infliximab 5 mg/kg IV at weeks 0, 2, then 6‑weekly – for severe/refractory PG
  - Dapsone 50–100 mg/day PO (for milder cases) – check G6PD first
  - Topical: clobetasol propionate 0.05% ointment BD; tacrolimus 0.1% ointment BD
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - Cyclosporine: avoid with grapefruit juice; interacts with azoles, macrolides, statins
  - Infliximab: contraindicated in active TB, severe infections; screen for latent TB before use
  - Prednisolone: caution in diabetes, hypertension, peptic ulcer disease; cannot stop abruptly
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - Cyclosporine – reduce dose if CrCl <30 mL/min; avoid if severe hepatic impairment
  - Prednisolone – no dose adjustment needed, but monitor glucose
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Starting high‑dose steroids without first ruling out infection** – can precipitate fulminant sepsis. Always send cultures + biopsy first. If in doubt, start broad‑spectrum antibiotics and delay steroids until culture results (usually 48 h).

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Secondary bacterial infection → cellulitis, abscess, sepsis
  - Pathergic extension of ulcer (debridement, surgery, trauma) → deep necrosis
  - Toxic megacolon (if active IBD)
  - Steroid side‑effects: hyperglycaemia, hypertension, psychosis, GI bleed
- **Chronic:**
  - Scarring, contractures, disfigurement
  - Recurrent disease (up to 40% relapse rate)
  - Development of underlying haematological malignancy (monitor FBC)

### Prognosis
- Most cases respond to systemic immunosuppression within 1–3 weeks; ulcers heal over weeks–months.
- Mortality low (<5%) but serious complications (sepsis, perforation) increase risk. Chronic course common if underlying disease untreated.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do a punch biopsy from the ulcer edge (including undermined border) for both histology and tissue culture – this is the cornerstone of diagnosis.**
- **Always consider underlying IBD or haematological disease – take a thorough history (bowel symptoms, blood, weight loss, joint pain, fever, night sweats) and check FBC, inflammatory markers, iron studies even if asymptomatic.**
- **Avoid any debridement, surgical excision, or even aggressive dressing changes – pathergy will worsen the ulcer dramatically. Use non‑adherent dressings and minimal handling.**

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT start high‑dose steroids before excluding infection – you risk turning a localised infection into life‑threatening sepsis.**
- **Do NOT diagnose PG simply because the ulcer “looks typical” – always biopsy and culture; mimics (especially infection and malignancy) are common.**
- **Do NOT manage PG without involving dermatology and, if IBD present, gastroenterology – this is a complex disease requiring multidisciplinary care.**

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** Ulcer rapidly enlarging (measure daily), new pus/fever/chills, severe pain not controlled, inability to tolerate oral intake, any sign of abdominal distension or change in bowel habit.
- **Follow‑up:** Dermatology within 1 week; if new‑onset IBD suspected – gastroenterology within 2 weeks. Check bloods weekly while on cyclosporine/prednisolone.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:** NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2 mmol/L, rapid ulcer expansion (>1 cm/day), fever >38.5°C without clear source, or any need for IV antibiotics.
- **Call ICU if:** Septic shock refractory to fluids/vasopressors, or toxic megacolon requiring surgical decompression.
- **Activate crash team for:** GCS <13, respiratory rate >35, systolic BP <90 mmHg despite 2 L IV fluid bolus.
